proposals.novelty: While competitive internal standards are standard in immunoassays (e.g., for hormone testing), their principled adaptation to the multi-step qRT-PCR workflow for direct sample correction is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from adding a synthetic spike-in only prior to amplification, as it corrects for losses from the very first step of extraction.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput qPCR Platform for Absolute Quantification of Multi-Gene Panels in Extracellular Vesicles Using Recombinant RNA Calibrants
proposals.motivation: High-throughput, absolute quantification of mRNA panels from complex samples like extracellular vesicles (EVs) is critical for robust biomarker discovery and validation. Current qPCR workflows, while sensitive, are limited in throughput and rely on relative quantification or single-gene absolute standards, hindering the analysis of coordinated gene expression networks. This gap is particularly acute in EV research, where sample volume is often limited and RNA content is low.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a platform utilizing a multiplexed set of sequence-specific recombinant RNA calibrants will enable the accurate, simultaneous absolute quantification of dozens of mRNA targets from limited EV samples in a high-throughput qPCR format.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a panel of 50 recombinant RNA calibrants, each containing the exact amplicon sequence for a target mRNA relevant to immune and urinary EV biology. These calibrants will be spiked in known concentrations into sample lysis buffer to control for extraction and reverse transcription efficiency. High-throughput qPCR will be performed on a microfluidic platform (e.g., BioMark HD). Data analysis will employ a novel calibration curve algorithm that normalizes target Cq values to the calibrant curve within each sample, enabling absolute copy number calculation for all targets simultaneously, adhering to MIQE 2.0 and MISEV guidelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, high-throughput method for absolute multi-gene quantification from EV and other limited samples, directly strengthening biomarker research. It will provide the field with a concrete framework and open-source analysis pipeline for implementing recombinant calibrants in complex panels, moving beyond single-gene assays.
proposals.novelty: While recombinant standards exist for single targets, their systematic integration into a high-throughput, multi-gene panel for absolute quantification, with built-in control for pre-amplification losses, represents a significant methodological advance beyond current MIQE-compliant practice.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Universal Recombinant RNA Calibrant for Multi-Target qRT-PCR in Extracellular Vesicle Research
proposals.motivation: The lack of standardized, universally applicable calibrants in qRT-PCR for extracellular vesicle (EV) research hinders inter-laboratory reproducibility and accurate quantification of biomarkers, as highlighted by the MIQE and MISEV guidelines. Current practices rely on separate, often inconsistent, standards for each target, complicating the analysis of complex, multi-gene signatures crucial for immune profiling or urinary EV diagnostics. A single, well-characterized calibrant would directly address this critical gap in methodological rigor.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, synthetic recombinant RNA molecule, engineered to contain multiple, non-homologous target sequences for key EV-associated genes, can serve as a universal calibrant to reliably normalize and quantify multiple qRT-PCR assays across different sample types and platforms.
proposals.method: We will design a chimeric RNA construct via in vitro transcription, incorporating 20-30 unique 80-120 nt sequences complementary to commonly assayed EV mRNA/miRNA targets (e.g., immune markers, urinary EV-associated genes). This construct will be spiked into EV lysates and biofluids at defined concentrations. Its performance will be rigorously validated against established MIQE 2.0 guidelines, assessing its impact on PCR efficiency, linearity, and limit of detection across multiple qPCR chemistries and instruments. Statistical analysis will compare inter-assay variability using the universal calibrant versus traditional, target-specific standards.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the EV and qPCR communities with a practical, MIQE/MISEV-aligned tool to dramatically improve the comparability of quantitative gene expression data. It will establish a foundational resource for high-throughput, multi-analyte studies, particularly in immune monitoring and urinary EV biomarker discovery, by enabling precise, parallel calibration.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic RNA standards exist for single targets, the creation of a multi-target, universal calibrant tailored for the complex matrix of EV samples is a novel concept. It integrates the principles of MIQE (quantification) and MISEV (sample-specific challenges) into a single, engineered solution, moving beyond general guidelines to a concrete, implementable standard.

proposals.title: A sample-specific reverse transcription efficiency correction model for extracellular vesicle RNA quantification using inverse curve analysis
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of RNA biomarkers in complex samples like extracellular vesicles (EVs) is critical for diagnostic applications. The reverse transcription (RT) step in RT-qPCR is a major, yet often overlooked, source of variability, as current MIQE guidelines treat RT efficiency (E_RT) as a constant or ignore it. This introduces systematic bias, especially when comparing samples with different inhibitors or RNA integrities, limiting the reliability of EV-based biomarker studies.
proposals.hypothesis: The discrepancy between parallel calibration curves generated from serially diluted DNA (amplicon) templates and in vitro transcribed RNA templates can be mathematically modeled to back-calculate a sample-specific RT efficiency for each target, correcting for sample-derived inhibition and RNA quality effects.
proposals.method: We will perform parallel RT-qPCR assays on urinary EV RNA samples and matched calibration series (DNA amplicons and in vitro transcribed RNA). For each target gene, we will fit four-parameter logistic curves to both calibration datasets. The systematic vertical offset (Cq difference) between the RNA and DNA curves across the dynamic range will be used to solve the inverse problem: E_RT = 10^(-1/slope_RNA) - 1, where the RNA curve's slope is corrected by the offset derived from the DNA standard. This sample- and target-specific E_RT value will then be applied to correct raw Cq values from biological samples using the formula: N0_corrected = N0_observed / (1 + E_RT).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, MIQE-aligned framework for incorporating sample-specific RT efficiency into qPCR data analysis, directly strengthening the rigor of EV-RNA quantification. It will yield a novel correction algorithm to improve inter-laboratory reproducibility and accuracy of low-abundance RNA measurements in clinical EV studies.
proposals.novelty: While MIQE 2.0 emphasizes reporting RT efficiency, current methods estimate it globally. Our approach innovates by treating the calibration curve discrepancy as an inverse problem to deduce a sample-specific efficiency, moving beyond the assumption of a constant E_RT and directly addressing sample-to-sample variability.

proposals.title: Dynamic Minimum Information Sequencing (dMISeq): A Real-Time, Context-Aware Framework for Adaptive Data Reporting and Quantification in Pathogen and EV Diagnostics
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostic sequencing and extracellular vesicle (EV) analysis follow a rigid, two-step paradigm where all samples undergo uniform deep sequencing and static bioinformatic quantification before reporting. This is inefficient, as simple samples (e.g., high pathogen load, low-complexity EV miRNA profiles) are over-processed, wasting resources, while complex samples may be under-interrogated. The field lacks a framework to dynamically adjust the depth and type of quantitative reporting based on the real-time biological context of the sample itself.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequencing system performing real-time, on-flowcell read normalization and complexity assessment can autonomously select an optimal, context-appropriate data reporting tier—from qualitative presence/absence to MIQE-compliant quantitative profiling—thereby optimizing workflow efficiency and informational yield without compromising diagnostic rigor.
proposals.method: We will develop a software architecture integrating real-time base-calling (e.g., Oxford Nanopore or Illumina DRAGEN) with a dynamic decision engine. This engine will implement algorithms for live estimation of sample metrics (e.g., pathogen-to-host read ratio, EV-associated gene detection frequency, Shannon diversity of miRNA species). Based on pre-defined thresholds aligned with MISEV and MIQE 2.0 guidelines, the system will trigger adaptive sequencing modes and automatically generate tiered reports, from minimal detection alerts to full quantitative datasets with appropriate normalization factors (e.g., stable non-coding RNAs for EVs) calculated in real-time.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a prototype 'sample-aware' sequencing framework that redefines the minimum information standard as a dynamic output. It will provide a novel methodology to drastically reduce time-to-answer and computational overhead for routine diagnostic and EV biomarker screening while ensuring MIQE/MISEV compliance. The project will yield a new guideline concept for context-dependent reporting in quantitative genomic analyses.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing real-time sequencing data analysis with the principles of established reporting guidelines (MIQE, MISEV) to create a closed-loop, adaptive instrument. It moves beyond static bioinformatics pipelines by making the reporting standard itself a dynamic function of the incoming data stream, a concept not yet realized in pre-2026 diagnostic or EV sequencing platforms.

proposals.title: MIQE-CRISPRdb: A Community-Curated Database for Standardized and Verified qPCR Assay Data
proposals.motivation: Despite the MIQE guidelines, qPCR assay data remains fragmented, poorly annotated, and irreproducible, wasting resources and undermining biomarker research, especially in complex sample types like extracellular vesicles. The field lacks a central, trusted repository where validated protocols can be shared and vetted, analogous to the pre-2026 problem of unvalidated CRISPR guide RNA data. This directly impedes the translation of qPCR findings into reliable clinical or research tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a structured, community-driven curation model, inspired by successful CRISPR resource databases, will create a self-correcting knowledge base that significantly improves the accessibility, reproducibility, and credibility of published qPCR assays.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based database with a structured submission form enforcing MIQE 2.0 core metadata (target, primers/probes, sample prep, instrumentation, analysis method). A backend algorithm will calculate a dynamic 'MIQE Confidence Score' for each assay entry, weighting factors like MIQE checklist completeness, number of independent verification uploads (with linked raw Cq data), and moderator-approved user ratings. The architecture will adapt features from pre-2026 community platforms (e.g., user profiles, version tracking, DOI integration) to incentivize and manage crowd-sourced curation.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first centralized, quality-filtered resource for qPCR assays, directly operationalizing the MIQE guidelines. It will accelerate assay selection and validation for researchers, particularly in emerging fields like urinary EV analysis, and establish a new standard for sharing reproducible qPCR data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the strategic adaptation of the community-curated database model from genome editing to the qPCR domain, and the formalization of a dynamic, multi-factorial credibility score to objectively rank assay reliability, moving beyond static checklists.

proposals.title: A Community-Driven, MIQE-Compliant Database for Validating Fragment Analyzer-Based NGS Library Quantification Across Diverse Library Chemistries
proposals.motivation: Accurate quantification of next-generation sequencing (NGS) libraries is critical for optimal sequencing performance, and the Fragment Analyzer (FA) with associated qPCR assays (FA-NGS workflow) is a widely adopted QC method. However, the proliferation of commercial and bespoke library preparation kits, each with unique adapter sequences and chemical compositions, creates uncertainty about the universal applicability and performance of FA-NGS qPCR assays. This lack of standardized, kit-specific validation data forces individual labs to perform redundant, costly verification experiments, hindering reproducibility and efficiency in NGS workflows, particularly in sensitive applications like extracellular vesicle RNA sequencing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of the FA-NGS qPCR quantification workflow is systematically influenced by specific attributes of NGS library chemistry (e.g., adapter sequence, polymerase, purification method) and that aggregating MIQE-compliant validation data in a public database will reveal predictable patterns, enabling robust pre-use suitability assessment and establishing universal quality standards.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured, web-accessible database using a PostgreSQL backend with a curated schema capturing MIQE 2.0-compliant qPCR parameters, library kit metadata (vendor, chemistry, adapter sequences), and performance metrics (sensitivity, linearity, PCR efficiency, precision). The core activity will be a systematic, multi-lab study generating the foundational dataset. Participating laboratories, following strict SOPs, will test a panel of 20+ diverse library chemistries (including Illumina, Twist Bioscience, and bespoke kits) using the FA-NGS workflow. Data upload will be via a standardized template, with automated checks for MIQE-required fields. The front-end will allow faceted searching by kit attributes and performance thresholds.
proposals.contribution: This work will transform NGS library QC by creating the first centralized, community-populated resource for FA-NGS assay validation, directly reducing redundant lab work. It will provide empirical evidence to guide kit selection and QC method application, enhancing reproducibility. By integrating with the MIQE and MISEV frameworks, it will extend the principles of rigorous reporting and standardization into the NGS library preparation domain.
proposals.novelty: While MIQE provides guidelines for qPCR experiments, and tools exist for NGS data analysis, no resource systematically links MIQE-compliant validation data to the specific chemical context of NGS library preparation kits to predict QC assay performance. This project's novelty lies in its community-driven, chemistry-focused approach to creating a predictive validation resource, moving beyond generic guidelines to actionable, kit-specific data.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating a Dynamic MIQE Training Platform: A Longitudinal Study on qPCR Reporting Adherence and Reproducibility Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of the MIQE guidelines, inconsistent and incomplete reporting of qPCR experiments persists, undermining reproducibility, especially in complex fields like extracellular vesicle (EV) research where pre-analytical variables are critical. Current training is static and its real-world impact on publication quality is unknown. This creates a critical gap between guideline existence and their effective implementation in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, evidence-based MIQE training and certification program will significantly improve reporting adherence among researchers, and that this improved adherence will be directly correlated with higher reproducibility scores in their subsequent publications.
proposals.method: We will develop an interactive online platform hosting modular MIQE training. Participants will complete certification by submitting structured metadata for a mock qPCR experiment. This cohort will be tracked longitudinally via automated publication alerts (e.g., using ORCID). Their post-certification papers will be analyzed for MIQE adherence using a Natural Language Processing (NLP) pipeline trained on the MIQE 2.0 checklist. Adherence scores will be statistically correlated with reproducibility indicators (e.g., journal impact factor, citation rates, retraction notices, and data availability). The platform will use this feedback to iteratively prioritize training modules addressing the most commonly neglected items.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver the first empirically validated training intervention for qPCR methodology, moving beyond advisory guidelines to an adaptive, impact-measured educational system. It will generate a unique dataset linking specific reporting behaviors to downstream research quality, providing actionable insights for strengthening the entire qPCR and EV biomarker research ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, data-driven design that uses real-world publication outcomes to iteratively optimize the training content itself, transforming MIQE from a passive checklist into an active, self-improving intervention system with measurable impact analytics.

proposals.title: A Data Cube and Workflow Framework for Automated, MIQE-Compliant qPCR Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite the MIQE guidelines, qPCR data analysis remains fragmented, often reliant on manual, error-prone spreadsheet workflows that obscure data provenance. This hinders reproducibility, especially in complex studies like extracellular vesicle (EV) biomarker research, which requires integrating qPCR with other data types. A robust, automated framework is needed to enforce standards and ensure traceability from raw fluorescence to final result.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling a qPCR experiment as a structured, multi-dimensional data cube and processing it with a geospatial-inspired, containerized workflow system, we can achieve fully automated, reproducible, and MIQE-compliant analysis with complete data provenance.
proposals.method: We will define a qPCR 'data cube' schema where dimensions are experimental factors (plate, row, column, run, sample type, target gene). A Nextflow pipeline, using Docker/Singularity containers for each step, will process this cube. Modules will implement established algorithms (e.g., baseline correction, Cq determination via sigmoidal curve fitting, efficiency correction via LinRegPCR, reference gene stability analysis via geNorm) in a traceable sequence. All parameters and data transformations will be logged in a structured JSON file aligned with MIQE 2.0.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, containerized analysis pipeline that enforces MIQE compliance by design, directly strengthening EV and molecular biomarker research. It provides a concrete computational framework that bridges the gap between MIQE/MISEV guidelines and daily lab practice, ensuring reproducible and auditable qPCR results.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and technical adaptation of geospatial data cube and workflow automation paradigms to the molecular biology domain, creating a unified, provenance-aware data structure for qPCR analysis that currently does not exist.

proposals.title: A Forensic Bioinformatics Platform for Automated Detection and Corrective Workflow Generation of MIQE Guideline Violations in qPCR Publications
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of the MIQE guidelines, compliance in published qPCR studies remains inconsistent, undermining the reliability and reproducibility of gene expression data, especially in complex fields like extracellular vesicle (EV) research. Current tools for guideline adherence are largely manual checklists or static validators, which are passive and do not provide actionable, computational solutions to rectify identified flaws. This gap necessitates an automated, forensic approach that not only audits published data but actively bridges the gap between error detection and correction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a workflow-driven bioinformatics platform can algorithmically detect MIQE violations from published qPCR metadata and, by generating executable, corrected analysis pipelines, provide a direct, reproducible path to data re-analysis that strengthens methodological rigor.
proposals.method: We will develop a Nextflow-based pipeline that ingests structured metadata from qPCR publications (e.g., from supplementary files). Core modules will implement rule-based algorithms (e.g., for missing technical replicates, inappropriate normalization, or absent PCR efficiency data) to flag violations. The platform's novel engine will then map these violations to corrective actions, automatically generating annotated Nextflow or Galaxy workflow scripts that implement MIQE-compliant re-analysis steps (e.g., proper outlier detection, reference gene stability analysis with geNorm or NormFinder, and correct efficiency correction models).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first active forensic bioinformatics tool for qPCR, moving from audit to remediation. It will provide a shareable 'audit trail' linking specific violations to concrete computational fixes, directly enhancing the reproducibility of qPCR data, particularly in biomarker discovery contexts like urinary EV studies. The platform will serve as an educational and quality-control resource for journals, reviewers, and researchers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of violation detection with the automated generation of executable, corrected analysis workflows, creating a proactive system for methodological remediation rather than a passive checklist. This integrates the principles of MISEV for EV research and MIQE 2.0 into an actionable, automated bioinformatics framework.

proposals.title: A DO-178C-Inspired Framework for the Formal Certification of Quantitative PCR Assays: Enhancing Reliability and Reproducibility in Biomarker Research
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of MIQE guidelines, the validation of qPCR assays in research, particularly for challenging sample types like extracellular vesicles (EVs), remains inconsistent and often insufficient for robust biomarker discovery. This variability undermines reproducibility and the translation of findings into clinical applications. A more systematic, engineering-based approach to assay validation is urgently needed to elevate the reliability of qPCR data in life sciences.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the structured safety and certification paradigms of aerospace engineering (specifically Failure Modes and Effects Analysis and DO-178C principles) to qPCR assay development will yield a formal 'Assay Certification' protocol that significantly improves assay robustness, inter-laboratory reproducibility, and traceability compared to current best-practice guidelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'qPCR Assay Certification' protocol structured around three phases: 1) **System Deconstruction & FMEA**: Mapping the entire qPCR process (sample prep, reverse transcription, amplification, analysis) to identify all potential failure modes (e.g., EV lysis inefficiency, RT inhibitors, primer-dimer formation). 2) **Redundant Control Implementation**: For each critical failure point, mandating specific, documented controls (e.g., synthetic spike-in RNAs for extraction/RTE, inter-plate calibrators, technical replicates as redundancy). 3) **Validation Checkpoints**: Defining quantitative performance thresholds (precision, accuracy, robustness) that must be met under simulated 'stress conditions' (e.g., different sample matrices, operator variability, reagent lot changes) to achieve 'certification'. The protocol will be tested and refined using urinary EV miRNA targets as a model system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the research community with a practical, step-by-step framework that moves beyond checklist-style guidelines to a demonstrable proof of assay reliability. It will directly strengthen the rigor of qPCR-based biomarker studies, especially in extracellular vesicle and immune-related research. The resulting 'certification dossier' concept will enhance data traceability and support more credible meta-analyses.
proposals.novelty: While MIQE provides essential reporting standards, this proposal innovates by applying proactive, system-level engineering safety analysis (FMEA) and a formal certification philosophy from aerospace to the *development process* itself, creating an actionable protocol for proving, rather than just stating, assay fitness-for-purpose.

proposals.title: Digital Signal Forensics for qPCR Data Integrity: An Algorithmic Framework to Infer MIQE Guideline Violations from Amplification and Melt Curve Fingerprints
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of the MIQE guidelines, ensuring methodological compliance in published qPCR data remains a manual, expert-dependent process, limiting reproducibility and meta-analyses. The inverse problem—inferring protocol violations from final data—is unsolved. This gap is critical in high-stakes fields like extracellular vesicle biomarker research, where Pfaffl's recent work integrates MIQE with MISEV, as flawed qPCR data undermines the validation of discovered biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: MIQE guideline violations leave statistically detectable fingerprints in the digital signal properties of qPCR amplification curves, melt curves, and derived metrics (Cq, efficiency), analogous to artifacts left by manipulation in digital multimedia.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline treating qPCR fluorescence data as time-series signals. Using a curated dataset of experiments with known MIQE compliance status, we will train machine learning models (e.g., Random Forests, 1D CNNs) to classify violations. Features will be engineered from signal properties (e.g., curve smoothness, derivative patterns, melt peak consistency) and metadata inconsistencies, adapting forensic concepts from steganalysis and audio authentication. Specific algorithms will target violations like inadequate replicates (via outlier detection in Cq distributions) and inappropriate baseline/threshold setting (via analysis of early amplification phase noise).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first automated, data-driven screening tool for qPCR data integrity, moving compliance checking beyond manual checklist auditing. It will provide a novel, objective layer of quality control for journal reviewers and meta-researchers, strengthening the reliability of qPCR-based findings in fields like immunology and urinary EV biomarker studies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying digital forensic and multimedia authentication paradigms to molecular biology data analysis, specifically to solve the inverse problem of deducing methodological flaws from result patterns, which has not been attempted systematically for qPCR.

proposals.title: MIQE-INT: A Consensus-Based Extension to the MIQE Guidelines for Reporting Quantitative PCR Data in Integrated Multi-Omics Studies
proposals.motivation: The integration of qPCR data with other omics layers (e.g., genomics, proteomics) is central to biomarker discovery in fields like extracellular vesicle research. However, pre-2026, there is no standardized framework for reporting the specific experimental design, pre-processing, and analytical steps required for such integration. This lack of standardization severely compromises the reproducibility, comparability, and reliability of published multi-omics findings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the methodology used to create the CONSORT-PRO extension for clinical trials will enable the successful development of a MIQE extension (MIQE-INT) through expert consensus. This extension will improve the completeness and reproducibility of reporting for qPCR data within integrated multi-omics studies.
proposals.method: We will follow a formal consensus development process, modeled on the creation of CONSORT extensions and the recent MIQE 2.0 revision. First, a steering committee will draft an initial checklist of proposed reporting items based on a systematic review of methodological challenges in pre-2026 multi-omics literature. This draft will then be iteratively refined through a multi-round Delphi survey involving an international panel of experts in qPCR, bioinformatics, and specific omics fields (e.g., extracellular vesicle genomics/proteomics). Consensus will be defined a priori, and the final MIQE-INT checklist will be validated by assessing reporting completeness in a sample of recent multi-omics publications before and after guideline dissemination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first community-vetted, domain-specific reporting standard for qPCR in integrated analyses, directly complementing existing general standards like MISEV. It will furnish journals, authors, and reviewers with a practical tool to enhance data quality and reproducibility, thereby strengthening downstream biomarker validation, particularly in complex sample matrices like urinary extracellular vesicles.
proposals.novelty: While MIQE provides general qPCR standards and MISEV covers EV-specific reporting, the novel adaptation of a clinical trial consensus model to solve a molecular reporting gap for multi-omics integration is a non-obvious, transdisciplinary solution. It formally addresses the unique analytical confounders introduced when combining qPCR with high-throughput omics data.

proposals.title: Direct RNA Quantification in Extracellular Vesicles: A Methodological Framework to Bypass Reverse Transcription and Validate Against MIQE-Compliant qPCR
proposals.motivation: Quantitative real-time PCR (qPCR) is the gold standard for RNA quantification but relies on a reverse transcription (RT) step, which introduces significant bias, inefficiency, and variability, particularly for low-abundance targets like extracellular vesicle (EV) RNA. This compromises the accuracy and reproducibility of biomarker studies. A method to quantify RNA directly would streamline workflows and enhance data fidelity, aligning with the MIQE and MISEV guidelines' core principles of rigor and transparency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific RNA-binding fluorescent probes or direct RNA-seq chemistries, when integrated into a thermocycling platform, can quantify specific RNA targets from urinary EVs with accuracy and precision comparable to MIQE-compliant RT-qPCR, thereby challenging the necessity of the cDNA conversion step.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged approach using pre-2026 technologies. First, we will adapt direct RNA-seq chemistries (e.g., Oxford Nanopore Technologies' direct RNA sequencing kit) for targeted quantification by developing a panel of strand-specific probes for EV-associated miRNAs (e.g., miR-21, miR-155) and mRNAs. Second, we will test fluorescent molecular beacon probes designed to bind and report on specific RNA sequences during thermal cycling without RT. All experiments will use well-characterized urinary EV samples, with results rigorously benchmarked against a MIQE 2.0-compliant RT-qPCR pipeline, employing Pfaffl's relative quantification model and variance component analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological framework for direct RNA quantification, potentially establishing a new best-practice guideline for EV-RNA analysis. It will deliver a concrete protocol and open-source analysis pipeline, contributing directly to the MIQE/MISEV ecosystems by addressing a key source of pre-analytical variability.
proposals.novelty: While direct RNA-seq exists for discovery, its application for targeted, quantitative analysis of specific EV-RNA biomarkers, with direct benchmarking against the established RT-qPCR gold standard, is novel. The proposal uniquely challenges a foundational step in molecular diagnostics by applying rigorous, guideline-driven validation from the qPCR field to an emerging technological alternative.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Digital Profiling of Extracellular Vesicle microRNAs via CRISPR-Cas13a and Ultrasensitive Microfluidics
proposals.motivation: Quantitative PCR (qPCR), the gold standard for nucleic acid quantification, is fundamentally limited by its requirement for target amplification, which introduces bias, artifacts, and complicates absolute quantification, especially for low-abundance targets like microRNAs in extracellular vesicles (EVs). Pre-2026, the EV and biomarker fields, guided by MISEV and MIQE, demand higher precision and reproducibility, which amplification-dependent methods struggle to provide for single-molecule sensitivity in complex biofluids like urine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating CRISPR-Cas13a's specific RNA recognition and trans-cleavage activity with a high-density microwell digital microfluidic platform can achieve absolute, amplification-free quantification of single RNA molecules directly from urinary EV lysates.
proposals.method: We will engineer a custom microfluidic chip with >100,000 picoliter wells to partition individual EV-derived RNA molecules. Each partition will contain a target-specific CRISPR-Cas13a complex and a quenched fluorescent reporter. Upon binding of a target miRNA, Cas13a's collateral cleavage activity will generate a localized fluorescent signal detectable via high-resolution fluorescence microscopy. We will develop a bespoke image analysis pipeline using a particle detection algorithm (based on TrackMate/Fiji) and a statistical model (Poisson distribution) to count positive partitions and calculate absolute molecule numbers, adhering to MIQE 2.0 principles for digital assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, amplification-free digital assay protocol for absolute miRNA quantification in EVs, directly strengthening the rigor of EV biomarker research as called for by MISEV. It will provide a new methodological cornerstone for high-confidence, low-input molecular diagnostics, moving beyond the limitations of qPCR and RT-dPCR.
proposals.novelty: While digital PCR exists, our approach is novel in completely inverting the need for enzymatic target amplification by leveraging the intrinsic signal amplification of Cas13a's collateral activity at the single-molecule level, specifically tailored for the challenging matrix of urinary EVs.

proposals.title: A Modular, High-Throughput Automated Platform for MIQE-Compliant RT-qPCR Analysis of Extracellular Vesicle-Associated Immune Gene Signatures
proposals.motivation: High-throughput biomarker discovery from complex samples like urinary extracellular vesicles (EVs) is bottlenecked by the manual, low-throughput nature of RT-qPCR workflows, which limits statistical power and reproducibility. The recent MISEV and MIQE 2.0 guidelines emphasize the need for standardized, high-quality data, yet no integrated automation platform exists to enforce these standards while scaling parallel processing of hundreds of samples from nucleic acid extraction to final quantification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a liquid handling robot with a modular software architecture that embeds MIQE 2.0 and MISEV compliance checks will enable fully automated, high-throughput RT-qPCR analysis of EV-associated immune genes, significantly improving throughput, reproducibility, and data standardization compared to manual methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a platform using a Hamilton Microlab STAR or equivalent liquid handling robot for automated RNA extraction from urinary EV samples, reverse transcription, and qPCR setup. A central software controller, built on an open-source framework like Django or Nextflow, will manage sample tracking and integrate with a cloud-based database. The system will employ specific algorithms for automated MIQE 2.0 compliance checking (e.g., flagging samples with missing efficiency or Cq confidence interval data) and MISEV-informed quality control (e.g., cross-referencing target genes with EV-associated markers). Parallel processing will be achieved through optimized deck layouts and pre-validated, low-volume reaction mixes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first fully automated, guideline-aware platform for high-throughput EV biomarker analysis, directly addressing a critical need in translational research. It will provide a validated, open-source software framework and protocol for generating MIQE 2.0/MISEV-compliant data at scale, enabling larger cohort studies in immunology and oncology with improved reliability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the seamless integration of domain-specific reporting guidelines (MIQE 2.0, MISEV) directly into the control software of an automated wet-lab workflow, creating a 'compliance-by-design' system for EV gene expression analysis, which is not a feature of existing commercial or open-source automation solutions.

proposals.title: Adapting Generalized Linear Mixed Models and Survival Analysis for Robust Normalization and Differential Expression in Urinary Extracellular Vesicle qPCR Studies
proposals.motivation: Quantitative PCR (qPCR) analysis of extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly from challenging biofluids like urine, is plagued by high technical and biological variability. Current MIQE-guided normalization methods (e.g., reference genes) often fail to adequately control for this noise, leading to unreliable biomarker discovery. Robust statistical frameworks from epidemiology, designed for heterogeneous population data, offer a promising but unexplored solution for improving quantification accuracy in EV-associated gene expression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying epidemiological models, specifically Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs) and time-to-event (survival) analysis principles, to qPCR data from urinary EVs will provide superior normalization and variance estimation, yielding more reproducible and statistically powerful detection of differentially expressed genes compared to standard ΔΔCq methods.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing urinary EV qPCR datasets (e.g., from transfusion studies) and generate new data from immune-challenged cohorts. Instead of simple ΔΔCq, we will model raw Cq values using a GLMM with fixed effects for experimental conditions and random effects for sample batch, donor variability, and RNA extraction efficiency. For longitudinal studies, we will adapt Cox proportional hazards models to treat amplification as a 'time-to-event' (detection), analyzing factors influencing amplification kinetics. Model performance will be benchmarked against MIQE 2.0-compliant standard analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source analysis pipeline that integrates epidemiological modeling with MIQE/MISEV standards, significantly improving the rigor of EV biomarker research. It will provide concrete guidelines for applying these models to qPCR data, enhancing the reproducibility and statistical power of studies on EV-associated genes from variable sample types.
proposals.novelty: While GLMMs are known in biostatistics, their targeted adaptation for qPCR normalization of EV RNAs, combined with the conceptual translation of survival analysis to amplification curves, represents a novel cross-disciplinary methodology not prescribed in current MIQE or MISEV guidelines.

proposals.title: A MIQE-Compliant, Microfluidics-Enabled Platform for Ultra-High-Throughput qPCR Profiling of Extracellular Vesicle-Associated Immune Genes in Large Population Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Population-level expression screening is crucial for discovering robust biomarkers and understanding disease heterogeneity, but is bottlenecked by the cost, labor, and sample volume requirements of traditional qPCR. This is especially true for complex, low-abundance samples like urinary extracellular vesicles (uEVs), which are rich in immune-related cargo but challenging to process at scale. Current methods lack the integration of rigorous pre-analytical standards (MISEV) with analytical reproducibility (MIQE 2.0) in a high-throughput format.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating automated microfluidic sample preparation for uEVs with a novel, multiplexed digital droplet qPCR (ddPCR) workflow will enable precise, MIQE-compliant quantification of immune gene panels from thousands of individuals, revealing population-scale expression patterns previously obscured by technical variability.
proposals.method: We will develop a system coupling an automated microfluidic platform for uEV isolation and RNA extraction from small urine volumes with a high-density ddPCR system. A custom primer/probe panel targeting 50 immune- and inflammation-associated genes will be designed. The workflow will be optimized using spiked-in synthetic oligonucleotides and reference samples to establish MIQE 2.0-compliant validation metrics (LOD, LOQ, PCR efficiency). Data analysis will employ a cloud-based pipeline for automated quality control (using the RDML data standard), normalization against a validated set of reference genes, and population-level expression distribution analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, end-to-end pipeline for population-scale EV-associated gene expression profiling, directly merging MISEV and MIQE guidelines into a practical high-throughput framework. It will generate the first large-scale dataset of uEV immune gene expression in a reference cohort, establishing normative ranges and variability. The platform will serve as a blueprint for reproducible, large-cohort molecular screening beyond uEVs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the seamless fusion of EV-specific pre-analytical rigor (MISEV) with the analytical precision of MIQE 2.0 within a dedicated ultra-high-throughput microfluidics/ddPCR architecture, a synthesis not yet achieved. This moves population qPCR from a bulk-tissue approach to a scalable, vesicle-specific liquid biopsy context.

proposals.title: A qPCR-based competitive inhibition assay for the systematic quantification of PCR inhibitors in complex biological samples
proposals.motivation: The reliability of qPCR-based biomarker studies, especially in challenging matrices like urine or blood-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs), is critically undermined by the presence of co-purified PCR inhibitors. Current MIQE guidelines emphasize reporting extraction efficiency but lack a standardized, quantitative method to directly measure inhibitor burden in individual samples. This gap leads to false negatives and inaccurate low-abundance target quantification, limiting the diagnostic utility of EV-associated nucleic acids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a competitive internal amplification control, co-extracted and co-amplified with target RNA, can be used to derive a quantitative Inhibition Coefficient (IC) that directly correlates with the concentration of PCR inhibitors in a sample, providing a more robust normalization factor than traditional reference genes alone.
proposals.method: We will adapt a duplex qPCR assay using a synthetic, non-competitive RNA spike-in (e.g., from the External RNA Controls Consortium) added during lysis of urinary EVs and immune cell samples. This control will be amplified with a primer/probe set distinct from target genes. By establishing a standard curve of the control's Cq shift in the presence of known inhibitors (e.g., heparin, salts, humic acids), we will calibrate the assay to report an IC value. Data analysis will employ a modified Pfaffl model, integrating the IC with reference gene stability (geNorm/ NormFinder) for a composite 'Inhibition-Corrected Relative Quantification' value.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, MIQE-compliant methodological framework to quantify sample-specific inhibition, moving beyond qualitative pass/fail assessments. It will directly strengthen the MIQE and MISEV guidelines by proposing a new mandatory reporting metric (the IC) for complex samples. The resulting protocol will enhance the reproducibility and accuracy of gene expression studies in EV research and immunology.
proposals.novelty: While inhibition is a well-known problem, the approach innovates by repurposing the qPCR assay itself as a direct sensor for inhibitors, rather than just for targets, and by generating a continuous, quantitative metric for inhibition that can be used for statistical correction, which is not addressed by current MIQE recommendations or standard spike-in controls.

ideas.title: MIQE-CRISPR: A Central Database with Community-Driven Curation for qPCR Assays
ideas.description: This project proposes to build a central, public repository for standardized qPCR assay and validation data by adapting the successful community-driven curation model from CRISPR genome editing databases. Analogous to platforms like the CRISPR RGEN Tools repository, which solved the problem of fragmented and unvalidated guide RNA data, the MIQE database would implement a structured submission framework requiring MIQE-compliant metadata (e.g., target, sample type, instrumentation, analysis method) and core validation metrics. The key adaptation is a community credibility system: each assay entry receives a dynamically calculated 'MIQE Confidence Score' based on factors such as the completeness of its MIQE checklist, the number of independent verification studies uploaded by other users, and curated peer feedback. This creates a self-correcting, crowd-verified knowledge base that incentivizes researchers to contribute high-quality, reproducible assay data, directly addressing the reproducibility crisis in qPCR by providing a single, trusted source for validated protocols.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Adaptive Sequencing: Dynamic Reporting and Quantification via Real-Time Read Normalization
ideas.description: This research challenges the static, two-step paradigm of quantification-then-sequencing-then-reporting by developing sequencing systems that perform real-time, on-flowcell read normalization. Crucially, the platform's live assessment of sample complexity, pathogen load, or host background is used to automatically select a context-appropriate data reporting tier—from minimal presence/absence to deep quantitative profiling—without user intervention. This creates a 'sample-aware' instrument that fundamentally redefines the minimum information standard as a dynamic output, contingent on the real-time data stream itself, thereby optimizing both operational workflow and informational yield based on immediate biological context.

ideas.title: Closed-loop adaptive NGS library preparation with real-time biosensor feedback
ideas.description: This approach integrates label-free biosensors directly into the library preparation workflow to perform real-time quality control on input nucleic acid fragments. The biosensor data—measuring parameters like fragment size distribution, integrity, and adapter-ligation efficiency—is then used to dynamically calculate and set optimal, sample-specific amplification conditions (e.g., cycle number, polymerase amount, or reaction time) in a closed-loop system. This prevents the blind over-amplification of poor-quality samples and the under-utilization of high-quality ones, maximizing library yield and uniformity while minimizing reagent waste and bias. The result is an adaptive, 'smart' preparation platform that tailors the biochemistry to the sample in real time, rather than applying a static, one-size-fits-all protocol.

ideas.title: A Community-Driven Database for FA-NGS Validation Across Library Chemistries
ideas.description: This project establishes a public, central database specifically designed to host MIQE-compliant qPCR validation data for the Fragment Analyzer-NGS (FA-NGS) workflow across a wide spectrum of commercial and bespoke NGS library preparation kits. By systematically testing and uploading performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity, linearity, precision) for each library chemistry, the community can populate a searchable resource that maps assay robustness to specific library attributes. This enables researchers to predict the suitability of the FA-NGS QC method for their specific library type before testing and identifies chemistries where the workflow may require optimization. Ultimately, the database shifts validation from a repetitive, isolated task into a collaborative effort that defines universal quality standards for NGS library quantification.

ideas.title: Adapting the CONSORT Extension for Multi-Omics Integration Reporting
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of standardizing reporting for qPCR integrated with other omics data by drawing an analogy from clinical trial reporting. In that field, the CONSORT-PRO extension was created to standardize reporting when patient-reported outcomes are added to trials. Adapting this solution, we would develop a MIQE-INT extension through expert consensus. This extension would create a modified checklist that adds mandatory reporting items for the design, pre-processing, normalization, and analysis specific to integrating qPCR data with genomics or proteomics data, thereby ensuring the reproducibility of multi-omics studies.

ideas.title: Automated Forensic Bioinformatics for qPCR: Workflow-Driven Detection and Correction of MIQE Violations
ideas.description: This research fuses bioinformatics workflow automation with algorithmic detection of MIQE violations to create a novel forensic bioinformatics platform. The system ingests published qPCR data and metadata, runs it through a Nextflow or Galaxy pipeline embedded with violation-detection algorithms to identify likely guideline non-compliance. Crucially, the platform's core synergy is its ability to automatically generate and propose corrected, executable workflow scripts that, if the original raw data were available, would re-analyze the data in a MIQE-compliant manner. This moves beyond passive detection to active remediation, creating a shareable, documented audit trail that directly links identified problems to concrete, reproducible solutions.

ideas.title: Adapting Geospatial Data Processing Pipelines for qPCR Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from geospatial data processing to the problem of automating and documenting qPCR analysis. In geospatial science, workflow systems like those built around GDAL and the Open Data Cube standardize and automate complex pipelines for satellite imagery, handling data provenance and reproducibility at scale. The adapted research idea applies this model by treating a qPCR experiment as a structured data cube, where samples have 'coordinates' in dimensions of plate, row, column, and run. A workflow management system (e.g., Nextflow) would be configured to execute analysis steps—from raw fluorescence processing to final differential expression—as modular, containerized processes on this cube. This ensures every result is traceable to its raw data and processing parameters, bringing the robust automation and documentation practices of geospatial analysis to molecular biology labs.

ideas.title: Machine learning-enhanced digital PCR for predictive NGS library QC and quantification
ideas.description: This research fuses machine learning-based melting curve analysis with the absolute quantification power of digital PCR to create an intelligent NGS library quality control platform. The system uses dPCR to partition and amplify library molecules, then applies ML algorithms to analyze the melting curves of individual partitions, extracting features related to fragment size, adapter dimer content, GC bias, and sequence complexity. This allows for the absolute quantification of not just total library concentration, but also of specific, quality-relevant subpopulations (e.g., perfectly ligated fragments vs. primer dimers). The resulting predictive model correlates these high-resolution melting signatures with final NGS metrics like cluster density, duplication rates, and coverage uniformity, providing a prescriptive diagnostic that can guide library re-purification or re-balancing prior to costly sequencing runs.

ideas.title: Adapting Aerospace Redundancy and Failure Mode Analysis to qPCR Validation
ideas.description: This idea proposes applying the rigorous, system-level safety and certification frameworks from aerospace engineering to the validation of routine research qPCR assays. In aerospace, systems are certified for flight through processes like Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) and standards like DO-178C, which mandate redundancy, predictable failure modes, and extensive documentation. Adapted to the research lab, this would involve developing a formal 'qPCR Assay Certification' protocol. This protocol would require researchers to systematically deconstruct their assay to identify all potential failure points (e.g., primer dimer formation, reagent degradation, pipetting error, instrument drift), implement documented controls and redundancies for each, and pass defined validation checkpoints for precision, accuracy, and robustness under expected 'operating conditions' (e.g., different sample matrices, operator skill levels). The output is not just a working assay, but a fully documented 'certification dossier' proving its reliability, making research qPCR more rigorous, reproducible, and traceable, akin to certifying an aircraft system for safe flight.

ideas.title: Forensic Data Provenance for qPCR: Detecting MIQE Violations via Digital Signal Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from digital forensics and multimedia authentication to the problem of detecting MIQE guideline violations from published qPCR data. In the analogous field, algorithms detect manipulation in digital images or audio by analyzing inherent signal properties, metadata inconsistencies, and statistical artifacts. The adapted idea applies this forensic mindset to qPCR, treating fluorescence amplification curves, melt curves, and reported Cq/efficiency values as digital signals. The developed algorithms will perform a 'forensic analysis' on this data to identify fingerprints of non-compliance, such as patterns indicative of inadequate technical replicates, inappropriate baseline threshold setting, or reagent lot inconsistencies. This creates an automated, data-driven screening tool that infers likely protocol violations from the statistical and signal properties of the results alone, addressing the inverse scaling problem of moving from final data back to probable methodological flaws.

ideas.title: Automated, Journal-Enforced QC for Single-Cell Data Submission
ideas.description: This idea develops a standardized, automated single-tube QC assay for single-cell or low-input NGS libraries that generates a machine-readable 'quality passport'. Upon manuscript submission, this data is ingested by journal systems, which must automatically verify its compliance with pre-defined, community-standard quality thresholds to flag potential issues. This inverts the traditional burden of proof, as the journal's platform must demonstrate a quantifiable QC failure to challenge submission validity, rather than the author providing extensive supplemental evidence. It enforces reproducibility at the point of submission by making robust, automated technical validation a mandatory and seamless part of the publishing workflow.

ideas.title: Melting Curve Spectral Fingerprinting for Contaminant Detection
ideas.description: This research expands melting curve QC by applying an analytical chemistry paradigm. The analogous problem of identifying unknown compounds in a mixture using spectral libraries is solved in spectroscopy by comparing full spectral signatures to reference databases. We adapt this solution by developing melting curve analysis not just for confirming target amplification, but as a diagnostic fingerprinting tool. The idea is to create a curated library of high-resolution melting curve 'spectra' for common PCR contaminants and adapter dimers. Using machine learning for pattern recognition, the complete shape and derivative plot of a sample's melting curve would be compared against this library. This would enable the detection of contaminants based on their unique melting signatures, even in mixed reactions, providing a powerful new layer of quality control for qPCR and next-generation sequencing library preparation.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Evidence-Based MIQE Training and Certification Platform with Real-Time Impact Analytics
ideas.description: This project establishes a comprehensive MIQE training and certification program that is uniquely integrated with a large-scale, longitudinal study tracking its effectiveness. The certification process will generate structured, report-level metadata from participants, which will be used to measure adherence and correlate it with reproducibility outcomes in subsequent publications. This data-driven feedback loop will allow for the iterative optimization of the training modules, focusing on the specific reporting items and practices that most strongly influence reproducibility. Thus, the platform evolves from a simple educational tool into an adaptive intervention system that empirically strengthens the methodological foundation of the qPCR field.

ideas.title: Lab-on-a-Chip for Point-of-Care qPCR Pooling and QC
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from environmental monitoring and space exploration, where complex chemical analyses have been miniaturized into portable, self-contained 'lab-on-a-chip' systems for use in remote locations. The analogous problem of performing sophisticated laboratory procedures in the field with minimal infrastructure is solved by integrating all steps—sample handling, reaction, and detection—onto a single microfluidic chip within a robust handheld device. Adapted to the original context, this leads to the design of a point-of-care device that miniaturizes the entire qPCR-based quality control and pooling workflow. The device would use microfabricated fluidic pathways to automate pool assembly from multiple input samples, perform thermal cycling in an ultra-compact reaction chamber, and conduct real-time fluorescence detection for quantification, all within a battery-operated, handheld unit suitable for field or bedside clinical use.

ideas.title: MIQE-Compliant Automated qPCR Workflow Platform
ideas.description: This project fuses lab automation with MIQE training to develop an integrated software and hardware platform. The system connects robotic liquid handlers to a central software suite that not only performs pooling calculations but also validates each step against MIQE guidelines in real-time. It generates a complete, immutable digital audit trail of the experimental process, including reagent lot numbers, calibration data, and environmental conditions, directly tied to the executed protocol. Successful completion of certified training modules unlocks advanced automation features, creating a direct link between researcher competency and the generation of inherently reproducible, high-quality qPCR data.

ideas.title: Adopt the Materials Data Curation Network (DCN) model for qPCR metadata
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the Materials Data Curation Network (DCN) in materials science to solve the FAIR data problem for qPCR experiments. The analogous DCN solution uses a formalized, expert-driven curation process with standardized vocabularies to ensure datasets are richly described and reusable. Adapted to qPCR, the research idea is to create a community-driven curation network that applies a standardized metadata template (based on MIQE principles) to qPCR datasets before public sharing. Expert curators would review and annotate submissions, ensuring raw data, experimental conditions, and analysis parameters are fully documented, interoperable, and ready for reuse, thereby operationalizing the FAIR principles for the qPCR community.

ideas.title: Hybrid Handheld Isothermal Amplifier with Cloud-Based Pooling and QC Analytics
ideas.description: This redesigned system uses a handheld device optimized for robust field operation, focusing solely on sample lysis and isothermal amplification to generate a fluorescent or colorimetric signal. The device acts as a data node, transmitting raw signal curves, sample metadata, and environmental readings to a secure cloud platform. There, a computational engine performs the complex tasks of quantitative analysis, statistical quality control, and virtual sample pooling by comparing the data against known assay performance models and population-level baselines. This hybrid approach maintains field portability while leveraging centralized computational power to deliver laboratory-grade result confidence and sophisticated pooling strategies that a standalone miniaturized qPCR device could not achieve.

ideas.title: A 'Sandbox' Framework for Novel qPCR Development: Adapting Regulatory Flexibility from Clinical Trial Design
ideas.description: This study investigates whether strict reporting checklists (like MIQE) inadvertently stifle the development of novel qPCR methods and analyses by enforcing a conformity that penalizes deviation. Drawing an analogy from clinical trial methodology—where rigid ICH-GCP guidelines were seen as a barrier to innovative adaptive or platform trial designs—we adapt the solution of regulatory 'sandboxes'. In clinical trials, bodies like the FDA created pilot programs allowing novel designs under special oversight, focusing on principled validation rather than checklist compliance. Adapted to qPCR, this research would design, implement, and evaluate a 'Methodological Innovation Sandbox' framework. Researchers could propose novel qPCR techniques or analyses that deviate from standard checklists, provided they collaboratively establish and meet alternative, fit-for-purpose validation criteria with reviewers. The study would empirically assess whether this structured-but-flexible approach increases the rate and quality of genuine methodological innovation in qPCR compared to the traditional strict checklist paradigm, while maintaining scientific rigor.

ideas.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Measure the Causal Effect of MIQE Guideline Implementation on Experimental Reproducibility
ideas.description: This study uses a prospective, multi-center randomized controlled trial to isolate the causal impact of MIQE adoption. Research groups conducting qPCR work are randomly assigned to an intervention group (receiving structured MIQE training, reporting templates, and audit support) or a control group (continuing with existing protocols). All groups submit their experimental designs and results for standardized, blinded independent replication attempts by a central core facility. The primary outcome is the rate of successful replication, directly comparing the intervention and control groups to quantify the causal effect of MIQE implementation on reproducibility, while controlling for confounding factors.

ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the established certification frameworks in Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics, this idea adapts the solution of tiered, competency-based personnel certification to the qPCR field. The analogous field solved the problem of ensuring technical competency and standardized practice through formal certifications with defined educational pathways, standardized exams, and continuing education, governed by professional bodies. The adapted research idea is to develop a structured MIQE certification program with multiple tiers (e.g., Practitioner, Lead, Auditor). This program would require candidates to complete specific training modules on the MIQE guidelines and qPCR best practices, pass both practical and written examinations, and maintain their certification through ongoing education. This ensures researchers possess a standardized, verifiable level of expertise, directly improving the reproducibility and reliability of qPCR data in research and diagnostic settings.

ideas.title: A Risk-Based, Tiered Reporting Standard for Scientific Data
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a universal 'minimum information' standard is the most practical approach for scientific reporting. Drawing an analogy from toxicology's risk-based, tiered testing strategies (like the Threshold of Toxicological Concern), the solution is to create a context-dependent reporting framework. The adapted research idea proposes a dynamic standard where the required depth of reporting is not fixed but scales with the study's assessed 'impact potential' or risk of irreproducibility. Factors such as methodological novelty, field of application (e.g., basic vs. clinical), and early community engagement (e.g., citation velocity) would determine the reporting tier. This would reduce unnecessary reporting burdens for routine or low-impact research while ensuring rigorous, information-rich documentation for high-stakes findings, making the overall system more efficient and adaptive.

ideas.title: Adapt Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) feedback control from polymer synthesis for real-time NGS library amplification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from polymer chemistry—using Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) sensors for real-time, feedback-controlled synthesis—to the problem of monitoring NGS library amplification. In the analogous field, QCM provides label-free, in-situ mass measurements to control polymer growth. The adapted research idea involves integrating a QCM biosensor, functionalized with sequence-specific capture probes, directly into the library amplification vessel (e.g., a PCR tube or chamber). As amplicons bind, the frequency shift provides a continuous, quantitative readout of library concentration. This data feeds into a control algorithm that modulates the amplification process in real-time, automatically stopping it or adjusting parameters once the optimal concentration is reached. This transforms library preparation from an open-loop, batch process with endpoint QC into a precise, closed-loop manufacturing step, improving yield consistency and reducing hands-on time and reagent waste.

ideas.title: Pre-Amplification Fragment Sorting via Microfluidic Dielectrophoresis
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard NGS workflow by performing quality control on initial nucleic acid fragments before library amplification to reduce wasted reagents. The analogy is drawn from semiconductor manufacturing, where optical/electrical inspection and sorting of components occurs early to remove defects before costly processing. The adapted solution is to implement a pre-amplification fragment sorting system using microfluidic dielectrophoresis or impedance cytometry. This integrated QC gate would assess fragment quality in a continuous flow, selectively routing only suitable material into the amplification chamber, thereby conserving expensive enzymes, nucleotides, and time by preventing the amplification of poor-quality or non-ideal input.

ideas.title: Reproducibility via Functional Equivalence and Blind Validation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology, where generic drugs gain approval via bioequivalence rather than full process disclosure, this project challenges the assumption that proprietary reagent identities are essential for reproducibility. The analogous solution involves establishing standardized performance criteria and blinded validation. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to develop and test a framework where experimental reproducibility is validated by demonstrating 'functional equivalence'—achieving statistically indistinguishable results using different reagents that meet clear, pre-defined performance specifications. A key component would be designing and piloting a blinded validation system, managed by a trusted third party, to certify reproducibility without compelling full reagent disclosure, thereby balancing open science with commercial interests.

ideas.title: Adaptive Optical Flow-Cell Sequencing for Real-Time Load Balancing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that pre-pooling quantification (e.g., qPCR) is necessary for balanced sequencing by drawing an analogy from telecommunications network management. In packet-switched networks, routers perform real-time adaptive load balancing to optimize data flow without pre-measuring packet volumes. The adapted solution is to develop a 'smart' sequencing platform with an adaptive optical flow-cell. This system would use real-time monitoring of cluster formation during the initial phases of a sequencing run to assess sample load. A feedback loop, akin to a network router's control plane, would then actively steer the sample or reagents to under-clustered regions of the flow-cell, performing in-situ normalization. This achieves balanced coverage directly from an unquantified, heterogeneous sample pool, rendering separate quantification steps obsolete.

ideas.title: Autonomous, Networked Point-of-Care Pooling System

The devices wirelessly transmit quantitative viral load data to a central software platform integrated with robotic liquid handlers in a core lab. The platform uses this real-time, geographically dispersed data to algorithmically calculate optimal pooling strategies and then automatically instructs the robots to execute the precise pooling and downstream testing. This creates a responsive, sample-to-answer network that maximizes testing throughput and efficiency by leveraging decentralized QC and centralized, automated execution.

ideas.title: Reproducibility Audit via Data-Driven Reagent Anonymity
ideas.description: This research direction combines the challenge to full reagent disclosure with algorithmic detection of guideline violations to create a data-driven audit framework. It will use machine learning models, trained on published qPCR data with known compliance status, to identify signatures of non-disclosure that actually hinder experimental replication. The core experiment will involve systematically re-running studies with 'anonymized' reagent protocols to see if the algorithmic predictions of irreproducibility are correct. The outcome is a principled, evidence-based standard for which reagent details are truly essential, moving the debate from principle to empirical proof.

ideas.title: Adaptive Oversampling of Rare Libraries via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that sequencing libraries must be pooled for even read representation. Drawing an analogy from conservation ecology, where survey effort is adaptively concentrated in high-probability niches for rare species rather than spread uniformly, we propose a solution for genomic studies. The adapted research idea involves developing pooling strategies that intentionally oversample rare libraries based on biological need. This is achieved by using a pre-sequencing assessment to model the 'biological niche' or priority of each library, then algorithmically weighting their contribution to the final pool to maximize information capture from rare, high-value states, thereby optimizing sequencing resources for biological insight rather than equal quantification.

ideas.title: MIQE-Adapt: An Intelligent, Tiered Framework for qPCR Guideline Education and Compliance
ideas.description: This project develops an intelligent software platform that integrates a simplified, educational MIQE-lite checklist with automated manuscript compliance checking. The core innovation is a tiered, adaptive rule engine that tailors its validation criteria and instructional feedback based on the declared context—such as a classroom, citizen science project, or clinical research lab. For students or low-resource users, it functions as an interactive educational tool, explaining the 'why' behind simplified requirements and progressively introducing complex MIQE items. For manuscript submission, it automatically applies the full MIQE checklist, but can also recognize and validate studies conducted under MIQE-lite constraints, providing nuanced compliance reports that acknowledge methodological context. This creates a continuous learning pathway from basic principles to full compliance, enhancing data quality across all levels of research.

ideas.title: Dual-mode self-referencing biosensor array with inline conditioning for specific, drift-corrected library monitoring
ideas.description: This redesigned system overcomes the critical flaw of non-specific signal interference in complex NGS library mixtures. It employs a microfluidic chip with a primary biosensor channel flanked by reference channels that quantify matrix and non-specific binding effects in real time. An inline passive dilution and buffer exchange module conditions nanoliter aliquots drawn from the amplification reaction to reduce interferent concentration before sensing. A dedicated processor runs a calibration-trained algorithm to dynamically subtract the reference signals from the primary sensor output, yielding a robust, specific measure of amplicon concentration throughout the library preparation process.

ideas.title: MIQE-Certified Blockchain Ledger for Credible and Auditable qPCR Science
ideas.description: This initiative fuses MIQE training with blockchain technology to create a credentialing and execution framework. Certified researchers would use a digital identity to log protocols and reagents onto a tamper-proof ledger, with each entry cryptographically linked to their certification status. The system can automatically flag actions that deviate from MIQE guidelines, providing immediate feedback and creating an immutable, auditable chain of custody for every experiment. This transforms certification from a static credential into an active tool for enforcing and verifying reproducibility in real-time, elevating trust in published qPCR data.

ideas.title: Tier-Locked, Machine-Actionable Reporting Standards with Pre-Registered Verification
ideas.description: This system replaces a voluntary, post-hoc tiered standard with a mandatory, pre-registered framework where the level of required methodological detail is explicitly chosen and justified before a study begins. The chosen tier (e.g., basic confirmatory, exploratory, high-stakes clinical) determines a specific, machine-actionable checklist of reporting items. Final study submissions, including code and data where applicable, must pass automated verification against this pre-registered checklist, ensuring compliance is enforced and transparent. This creates a practical, scalable system that adapts to context while preventing the degradation of reporting quality by making the chosen standard auditable and integral to the publication pipeline.

ideas.title: Instrument-Verified Chain of Custody for qPCR Protocol Fidelity
ideas.description: This system establishes a cryptographically secure, machine-verifiable record of qPCR protocol execution by automating data capture at the point of action. IoT-enabled instruments and reagent scanners generate digitally signed logs for critical steps like pipetting volumes, thermal cycling parameters, and reagent vial scans. These signed event streams are aggregated into a protocol execution record, which is then immutably timestamped and anchored via a blockchain or distributed ledger. The result is a provenance record that links the digital attestation directly to instrument operations, providing evidence of protocol adherence rather than just the preservation of a planned method.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Field Guides' Approach from Ecology to Create a Tiered qPCR Quality Framework
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'MIQE-lite' concept by drawing an analogy from ecology, where simplified, tiered field guides are successfully used for education and citizen science. The analogous solution involves creating accessible, decision-tree-based identification tools that prioritize robust, observable criteria over exhaustive professional details. Adapted to qPCR, this means developing a 'qPCR Field Guide' system—not a single checklist, but a tiered framework. It would include a basic flowchart for classroom use (emphasizing core principles), a modular, customizable checklist for low-resource or citizen science applications, and clear pathways to the full MIQE guidelines for deeper learning. This approach scales down complexity in a context-sensitive way, making rigorous practice more accessible without abandoning the underlying standards.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed and Self-Supervised Machine Learning for Robust Melting Curve Diagnostics
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines a physics-informed neural network, which incorporates fundamental biophysical equations of DNA melting as a regularization term, with a self-supervised learning framework that pre-trains on vast amounts of unlabeled curve data. This dual strategy drastically reduces the dependency on scarce, perfectly annotated clinical datasets by ensuring predictions are thermodynamically plausible and by learning instrument-invariant features. The resulting system is more robust to batch effects, reagent variability, and novel sample types, enabling reliable QC diagnostics even with limited labeled examples from a new setting.

ideas.description: This research direction develops an integrated software and robotic system that not only automates complex experimental workflows, like sample pooling, but also immutably logs every parameter, calculation, and physical action. This enables a fundamental shift in validation: instead of authors laboriously proving compliance with reporting standards, the journal's role is to interrogate this executable record to identify any deviations from claimed methods. The synergy transforms validation from a subjective, document-based exercise into an objective, data-driven audit of the actual automated process.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising for High-Resolution Melting Curve Analysis
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from geophysics to the original problem of interpreting complex melting curves. In seismology, 1D convolutional neural networks (CN1D-CNNs) are successfully employed to denoise seismic waveforms, isolating critical signal features from noise. We adapt this by training a similar 1D-CNN architecture on high-resolution melting (HRM) data. The network will learn to filter out instrument noise, baseline artifacts, and non-specific fluorescence, producing a 'clean' melting curve derivative. This denoised output will then serve as a superior input for downstream machine learning classifiers (e.g., for mutation detection or pathogen identification), significantly improving the resolution and reliability of QC diagnostics in molecular biology.

ideas.title: Adapting Regulatory 'Presumption of Compliance' to Journal Data Policy Enforcement
ideas.description: This idea inverts the burden of proof for research validity by shifting responsibility from authors to journals, analogous to how pharmaceutical GMP regulation works. In that field, certified facilities are presumed compliant, and regulators bear the burden of proving specific violations. Adapting this, the research idea proposes that journals first certify institutional research data management systems. Once certified, submissions from those institutions are accepted as policy-compliant by default. The journal must then actively audit and provide evidence to challenge any specific submission, moving from a model of universal author proof to one of targeted journal-led verification of non-compliance.

ideas.title: Third-Party Pre-Publication Compliance Audit
ideas.description: This system mandates that all manuscripts accepted for publication must undergo an automated and human-supervised audit by an independent, non-profit compliance authority. The auditor verifies data availability, ethical approvals, conflict of interest disclosures, and statistical reporting against journal-specific rules using standardized protocols. The journal's responsibility shifts to requiring and publishing this auditor's certificate, which serves as the definitive proof of compliance. This removes the burden from the author at the point of submission and the perverse incentive from the journal, while creating a centralized, expert body accountable for verification integrity.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Closed-Loop Control for Automated Sample Pooling
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of connecting a software toolkit to robotic liquid handlers by introducing adaptive, closed-loop control inspired by precision agriculture systems. In that field, sensor networks provide real-time data (e.g., soil moisture) to a central controller, which dynamically adjusts irrigation execution. Adapted to the lab automation context, the software toolkit would be integrated not only to command the robots but also to receive continuous feedback from in-line sensors on the liquid handling platform (e.g., for volume, concentration, or droplet detection). This creates a fully automated, responsive system where pooling calculations are dynamically updated based on real-time input, enabling error correction, protocol optimization, and hands-off execution of complex, variable-sample pooling workflows.
